title: Data from: The bacterial antimonite oxidase AnoA: unexpected diversity and environmental widespread occurrence
author: DA COSTA, Claire
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The growing contamination of urban areas by antimony (Sb) has sparked interest in microbial processes that modulate Sb speciation in ecosystems. The bacterial antimonite oxidase AnoA is the only oxidase known so far   whose gene expression is specifically   induced by Sb(III), but its annotation in public databases is currently lacking. Here, the computational search for AnoA orthologs predicted an unexpected phylogenetic distribution across the   Pseudomonadota   and   Actinomycetota  . Putative orthologs were identified in both known Sb(III)-oxidizers (  e.g.  ,   Shinella  ,   Hydrogenophaga  ,   Bosea  ,   Cupriavidus  ,   Pseudomonas  ) and taxa not previously linked to the Sb cycle (  e.g.  ,   Bradyrhizobium  ,   Mesorhizobium  ,   Methylobacterium  ,   Paraburkholderia  ). The   anoA   gene is single-copy in most Proteobacterial genomes, but often detected in multiple copies in the   Actinomycetota  . Furthermore, sequence evolutionary distances suggest that it is mainly inherited vertically, with horizontal transfer events, in particular towards the   Gammaproteobacteria  . Using the constructed database, new PCR primers were designed and outperformed existing strain-specific primers in amplifying the   anoA   gene from samples with varying Sb levels and microbial profiles. Sequencing and quantification of PCR amplicons revealed a diverse range of sequence in sediments and natural biofilms, indicating that the oxidase is more environmentally diverse and widespread than previously thought and may play a significant role in Sb(III) detoxification.
